title: Predicting wave interference patterns
description: Practice predicting how two wave pulses interfere to form new wave patterns as they pass through each other.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two wave pulses move towards each other as shown below.  The pulses have the same width, but the pulse on the right has less amplitude. 

[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the resulting wave pattern when the centers of the two pulses meet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When two waves overlap, their individual displacements add up to produce the resulting wave pattern.  Their combined wave structure can have constructive or destructive interference sections.
Let's start with the displacements of the $\blueD{\text{left wave pulse}}$ and then add the displacements of the $\greenD{\text{right wave pulse}}$ to produce the combined interference pattern:

[[☃ image 1]]

Since both wave pulses have upwards displacements, the two wave pulses will $\greenD{\text{constructively interfere}}$ (highlighted by the $\greenD{\text{arrows}}$) for more positive amplitudes.
The correct answer is

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Two wave pulses move towards each other as shown below. The pulses have the same amplitude and width. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What wave pattern results when the centers of the two pulses meet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When two waves overlap, their individual displacements add up to produce the resulting wave pattern.  Their combined wave structure can have constructive or destructive interference sections.
Let's start with the displacements of the $\blueD{\text{left wave pulse}}$ and then add the downwards (negative) displacements of the $\goldD{\text{right wave pulse}}$ to produce the combined interference pattern:

[[☃ image 1]]

 Since the wave pulses have opposite displacement directions, the two wave pulses will $\goldD{\text{destructively interfere}}$ (highlighted by the $\goldD{\text{arrows}}$) for reduced amplitudes.

The correct answer is

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Two wave pulses move towards each other as shown below. The pulses have the same width, but the pulse on the right has less amplitude. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What wave pattern results when the centers of the two pulses meet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When two waves overlap, their individual displacements add up to produce the resulting wave pattern.  Their combined wave structure can have constructive or destructive interference sections.
Let's start with the displacements of the $\blueD{\text{left wave pulse}}$ and then add the downwards (negative) displacements of the $\goldD{\text{right wave pulse}}$ to produce the combined interference pattern:

[[☃ image 1]]

 Since the wave pulses have opposite displacement directions, the two wave pulses will $\goldD{\text{destructively interfere}}$ (highlighted by the $\goldD{\text{arrows}}$) for reduced amplitudes.

Also, the center of the interference pattern still has upwards displacement because the the left pulse had more upwards displacement than the right pulse at their centers.

The correct answer is

[[☃ image 1]]

 Since the wave pulses have opposite displacement directions, the two wave pulses will $\greenD{\text{destructively interfere}}$ (highlighted by the $\greenD{\text{arrows}}$) for reduced amplitudes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two wave pulses move towards each other as shown below. 

[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the resulting wave pattern after the two pulses meet and then move apart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When two waves overlap, their individual displacements add up to produce an interference pattern. After the waves move apart, they continue traveling in the original direction with the same shape.
After the waves move apart, the wide wave continues to travel to the left and the tall wave travels to the right  
The correct answer is

[[☃ image 1]]
